lesson_title: The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.text: The {{c1::override}} specifier is placed after a function signature to indicate it should override a base class virtual function.
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.front: What happens if you use the override specifier but the function doesn't actually override anything?
cards.back: The compiler will generate an error, helping you catch mistakes where the signature doesn't match exactly.
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.text: To mark a virtual function as an override: {{c1::virtual std::string getName() const override;}}
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Compilation error: func() in Derived doesn't override anything because it's missing const.
cards.explanation: The override specifier causes a compile error when the signature doesn't match. Without override, this would silently create a new function instead of overriding.
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.front: What does the final specifier do when applied to a virtual function?
cards.back: It prevents derived classes from overriding that function. Any attempt to override will cause a compilation error.
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.text: To prevent a function from being overridden further: {{c1::virtual void func() final;}}
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.front: What does the final specifier do when applied to a class?
cards.back: It prevents any other class from inheriting from that class. The class cannot be used as a base class.
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.text: To prevent a class from being inherited from: {{c1::class MyClass final \{ \};}}
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.back:
A special exception where an override can return a pointer or reference to a derived type instead of the base type.

Example: If Base::func() returns Base*, Derived::func() can return Derived*.
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.question: Is this valid C++ code?
cards.explanation: This uses covariant return types. Derived::clone() returns Derived* instead of Base*, which is allowed because Derived* is a more specific type derived from Base.
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.concept: Why you should always use the override specifier
cards.explanation:
The override specifier catches common mistakes at compile time:
  • Misspelled function names
  • Mismatched const qualifiers
  • Wrong parameter types
  • Base function signature changes
Without override, these mistakes create new functions instead of overriding, causing subtle bugs.
examples: // Without override - silently creates new function:
virtual void Draw() { }  // Oops, should be draw()
virtual void Draw() override { }  // Error: no base function to override
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

cards.concept: When to use the final specifier
cards.explanation:
Use final when:
  • Further overriding would break class invariants
  • Performance is critical (allows devirtualization)
  • The implementation is complete and shouldn't be changed
Use sparingly - it limits extensibility and violates the open-closed principle.
examples: virtual void commit() final { /* critical logic */ }
class OptimizedCache final {  // No further derivation
cards.source: 25.3 - The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types
